to project high-dimensional embeddings into 2D or 3D space so humans can visually explore structure, clusters, and relationships in the data.
“Visualization techniques like t-SNE, UMAP, and Nomic Atlas help you
project these high-dimensional embeddings into 2D space for easy exploration on a flat surface
is a numeric representation of data in a high-dimensional vector space, where:
: each word is a vector (e.g., 300 dimensions) capturing semantic similarity
: each document is a vector representing its meaning
: each image is a vector capturing visual features
directly because they’re in high-dimensional space (e.g., 128, 256, 300, 1024 dimensions).
- See if similar concepts cluster together - Check if the embedding space is structured meaningfully

How t-SNE and UMAP work for embedding visualization
methods that preserve local structure, making them ideal for embedding visualization.

: t-SNE and UMAP applied to word embeddings from 19th-century literature.
---
- Word embeddings (e.g., from Word2Vec, BERT) - Document embeddings (e.g., from transformer models) - Image embeddings (e.g., from CNNs) - Latent space from autoencoders
- Get the embedding matrix (N × d, where N is number of items, d is embedding dimension)
```python from sklearn.manifold import TSNE from umap import UMAP
# t-SNE tsne = TSNE(n_components=2, perplexity=30) embedding_2d = tsne.fit_transform(embeddings)
# UMAP umap = UMAP(n_neighbors=20, min_dist=0.3, metric='correlation') embedding_2d = umap.fit_transform(embeddings) # [] ```

- Use scatter plots (matplotlib, seaborn, plotly) - Color by labels, categories, or metadata - Example: clustering of myeloid, T-cells, cancer, B-cells, epithelial cells
5.
: - Look for clusters - Check if semantic groups separate - Identify outliers or mixed regions
Embedding visualization in Classical ML vs. Generative AI

in high-dimensional data
- Purpose: - Data exploration - Debugging models - Understanding feature spaces
(e.g., token embeddings from BERT, GPT) - Understanding

in recommendation systems
- Purpose: - Debugging and improving generative models - Understanding how models organize knowledge - Explaining model behavior to stakeholders
are the go-to tools for making embeddings interpretable.

t-SNE (t-distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding) operates in two stages. First, it converts pairwise Euclidean distances between high-dimensional points into conditional probabilities that represent similarities. The probability that point i would pick point j as a neighbour is proportional to a Gaussian centred at i:

The bandwidth σ_i is chosen per-point so that the effective number of neighbours (perplexity) matches the user-specified target. High perplexity = wider Gaussian = considers more distant points as neighbours.

In the low-dimensional space, t-SNE uses a Student t-distribution with 1 degree of freedom (heavy tails) to define similarities. The heavy tail prevents crowding — in 2D there is simply less room than in 1024D, so nearby points would pile up with a Gaussian. The t-distribution spreads them out:
t-SNE then minimises the Kullback-Leibler divergence between the high-dimensional distribution P and the low-dimensional distribution Q using gradient descent:

UMAP (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection) is grounded in Riemannian geometry and algebraic topology. Its core insight is that data lies on a manifold — a lower-dimensional curved surface embedded in high-dimensional space — and that manifold can be approximated by a fuzzy simplicial set (a weighted graph).

UMAP constructs this graph by finding k nearest neighbours for each point, then assigns edge weights using a normalised Gaussian that adapts to local density. Crucially, it normalises so that the nearest neighbour always has weight 1.0 regardless of its actual distance — this makes the representation invariant to local density variation:

where ρ_i is the distance to the nearest neighbour and σ_i is chosen to achieve the target number of neighbours. UMAP then optimises a low-dimensional layout that minimises the cross-entropy between the high-dimensional fuzzy graph and the low-dimensional one, using binary cross-entropy on edge probabilities rather than KL divergence.
A t-SNE or UMAP plot shows in seconds whether a model's embeddings separate classes cleanly — something that would take hours of quantitative benchmarking to establish otherwise.

Mislabelled training examples appear as isolated points in the wrong cluster. Duplicate documents collapse into a single dense cluster. Both are invisible in quantitative metrics but obvious in a 2D plot.
If a semantic search system returns unexpected results, plotting the query vector and top-k document vectors reveals whether the issue is embedding quality or index configuration.
A scatter plot coloured by topic or sentiment is immediately interpretable by product managers and business stakeholders — no ML background required.
If embeddings from a fine-tuned model show cleaner clusters than the base model, visualisation confirms that fine-tuning is beneficial before running expensive downstream evaluations.

Distances between clusters in a t-SNE plot are meaningless. Two clusters that appear far apart may be closer in the original space than two clusters that appear adjacent. Never use t-SNE to judge inter-cluster distances.

Different perplexity values (t-SNE) or n_neighbors values (UMAP) can produce dramatically different layouts. A 'good-looking' plot is not a reliable metric; always show multiple hyperparameter settings.
There is no function from 2D back to the original space. Visualisation is for human inspection only, not for downstream computation.

t-SNE is O(n² log n) naive or O(n log n) with Barnes-Hut approximation. For more than 50,000 points, use UMAP (roughly O(n^1.14) empirically) or first reduce to 50 dimensions with PCA, then apply t-SNE.
Both t-SNE and UMAP use random initialisation and stochastic optimisation. Fix the random seed (random_state=42) for reproducible figures.

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np

# Example: embed 1000 news headlines
headlines = [
    "Central bank raises interest rates for third consecutive quarter",
    "Tech giant announces record quarterly profits amid layoffs",
    "Scientists discover new exoplanet in habitable zone",
    # ... more examples
]
labels = ["finance", "technology", "science"]  # one per headline

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")  # 384-dim embeddings
embeddings = model.encode(headlines, normalize_embeddings=True)
print(embeddings.shape)  # (1000, 384)

from sklearn.manifold import TSNE
from umap import UMAP
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
import numpy as np

embeddings = np.load("embeddings.npy")  # (N, 384)

# Option A: t-SNE (good cluster separation, distorts global distances)
tsne = TSNE(
    n_components=2,
    perplexity=30,       # effective number of neighbours; try 5–50
    n_iter=1000,
    random_state=42,
    metric="cosine",     # match your similarity metric
    init="pca",          # PCA init is more stable than random
)
coords_tsne = tsne.fit_transform(embeddings)  # (N, 2)

# Option B: UMAP (faster, preserves more global structure, supports new points)
umap_model = UMAP(
    n_components=2,
    n_neighbors=15,      # local neighbourhood size; try 5–50
    min_dist=0.1,        # how tightly packed clusters are; 0.0–0.5
    metric="cosine",
    random_state=42,
)
coords_umap = umap_model.fit_transform(embeddings)  # (N, 2)
# To embed new points later: coords_new = umap_model.transform(new_embeddings)

# Option C: PCA first, then t-SNE (fast for large datasets)
pca = PCA(n_components=50, random_state=42)
reduced = pca.fit_transform(embeddings)  # (N, 50)
coords_pca_tsne = TSNE(n_components=2, perplexity=30, random_state=42).fit_transform(reduced)

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.cm as cm
import numpy as np

def plot_embeddings(coords: np.ndarray, labels: list[str], title: str) -> None:
    """Scatter plot of 2D coordinates, coloured by label."""
    unique_labels = sorted(set(labels))
    colours = cm.tab10(np.linspace(0, 1, len(unique_labels)))
    label_to_colour = dict(zip(unique_labels, colours))

    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8))
    for label in unique_labels:
        mask = np.array(labels) == label
        ax.scatter(
            coords[mask, 0], coords[mask, 1],
            c=[label_to_colour[label]],
            label=label,
            s=8,
            alpha=0.6,
        )
    ax.legend(markerscale=3, loc="upper right")
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.axis("off")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(f"{title.lower().replace(' ', '_')}.png", dpi=150)
    plt.show()

plot_embeddings(coords_tsne, labels, "News Headlines — t-SNE")
plot_embeddings(coords_umap, labels, "News Headlines — UMAP")

import numpy as np
from sklearn.manifold import TSNE
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Classic word analogy example: king - man + woman ≈ queen
words = [
    "king", "queen", "man", "woman",
    "Paris", "London", "France", "England",
    "cat", "dog", "fish", "bird",
]

# Use a pre-trained model (gensim Word2Vec) to get 300-dim word vectors
# In practice: pip install gensim; download Google's 300d model
# from gensim.models import KeyedVectors
# model = KeyedVectors.load_word2vec_format("GoogleNews-vectors-negative300.bin", binary=True)
# vecs = np.array([model[w] for w in words])

# Simulated embeddings that capture the structure
np.random.seed(42)
royalty = np.random.randn(4, 50)   # king, queen, man, woman — one cluster
cities  = np.random.randn(4, 50) + 5
animals = np.random.randn(4, 50) + 10
vecs = np.vstack([royalty, cities, animals])  # (12, 50)

tsne = TSNE(n_components=2, perplexity=3, random_state=42)
xy = tsne.fit_transform(vecs)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
colours = ["blue"]*4 + ["green"]*4 + ["red"]*4
ax.scatter(xy[:, 0], xy[:, 1], c=colours, s=100)
for i, word in enumerate(words):
    ax.annotate(word, xy[i], fontsize=10, ha="center", va="bottom")
ax.set_title("Word Embeddings — t-SNE (perplexity=3)")
ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
Example 2: Visualising Document Clusters for RAG Debugging

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
from umap import UMAP
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# Simulate a RAG knowledge base with 3 topic groups
docs = {
    "database": [
        "PostgreSQL supports ACID transactions and complex joins.",
        "MongoDB is a document-oriented NoSQL database.",
        "Redis is an in-memory key-value store for caching.",
        "Indexing strategies determine query performance in relational databases.",
    ],
    "ml": [
        "Neural networks learn via backpropagation and gradient descent.",
        "Random forests combine multiple decision trees via bagging.",
        "Support vector machines find the maximum-margin hyperplane.",
        "Gradient boosting builds models sequentially on residuals.",
    ],
    "web": [
        "HTTP is a stateless protocol for client-server communication.",
        "REST APIs use HTTP verbs: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE.",
        "WebSockets enable full-duplex communication over a single TCP connection.",
        "CORS policies control which origins can access browser APIs.",
    ],
}

all_docs = []
all_labels = []
for topic, texts in docs.items():
    all_docs.extend(texts)
    all_labels.extend([topic] * len(texts))

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
embeddings = model.encode(all_docs, normalize_embeddings=True)

# Add a query to see where it lands in the embedding space
query = "How do I optimise slow database queries?"
q_emb = model.encode([query], normalize_embeddings=True)
all_points = np.vstack([embeddings, q_emb])
all_labels.append("QUERY")

# UMAP: supports transform() so we can add new queries later
umap = UMAP(n_components=2, n_neighbors=5, min_dist=0.2, metric="cosine", random_state=42)
xy = umap.fit_transform(all_points)

colours = {"database": "#3b82f6", "ml": "#10b981", "web": "#f59e0b", "QUERY": "red"}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
for label in set(all_labels):
    mask = np.array(all_labels) == label
    size = 200 if label == "QUERY" else 50
    marker = "*" if label == "QUERY" else "o"
    ax.scatter(xy[mask, 0], xy[mask, 1], c=colours[label], label=label, s=size, marker=marker)
ax.legend()
ax.set_title("RAG Knowledge Base — UMAP\nQuery lands near the 'database' cluster")
ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
# Confirm: query should land near the database cluster — if it lands elsewhere,
# your embedding model doesn't understand this domain.
Real-World Example: Auditing a Customer Support Embedding Index

A team maintains a semantic search index over 50,000 customer support tickets embedded with a sentence transformer model. Users report that searches for billing questions occasionally return shipping answers. The team uses UMAP to audit the embedding space.

import numpy as np
from umap import UMAP
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.cluster import DBSCAN

# Assume: embeddings (50000, 768), labels (50000,) with values like 'billing','shipping','account'
# embeddings = np.load("support_embeddings.npy")
# labels = np.load("support_labels.npy")

# For reproducibility, simulate the problem
np.random.seed(0)
N = 500
billing  = np.random.randn(N//2, 50) + np.array([0]*50)
shipping = np.random.randn(N//4, 50) + np.array([2]*50)
# Simulate overlapping region: some shipping tickets mislabelled as billing
mixed    = np.random.randn(N//4, 50) + np.array([1]*50)

embeddings = np.vstack([billing, shipping, mixed])
labels = (["billing"] * (N//2)) + (["shipping"] * (N//4)) + (["billing"] * (N//4))

umap = UMAP(n_components=2, n_neighbors=15, min_dist=0.05, metric="cosine", random_state=42)
xy = umap.fit_transform(embeddings)

colours = {"billing": "#3b82f6", "shipping": "#f59e0b"}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 7))
for label in ["billing", "shipping"]:
    mask = np.array(labels) == label
    ax.scatter(xy[mask, 0], xy[mask, 1], c=colours[label], label=label, s=8, alpha=0.5)
ax.legend()
ax.set_title("Support Ticket Embeddings — UMAP\nOverlap region reveals mislabelled billing/shipping tickets")
ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Finding: the UMAP plot shows a dense overlap region between billing and shipping.
# Manually inspecting those tickets reveals that 'refund for a shipped item' tickets
# are labelled as billing but their embeddings sit in the shipping cluster.
# Fix: re-label the overlap region and retrain the classifier.
t-SNE does NOT preserve distances between clusters. Two clusters being far apart in the plot does not mean they are semantically distant. Use UMAP if you need to reason about global structure.
Using perplexity too high or too low:
Perplexity below 5 creates noisy, disconnected scatter. Above 100, everything merges into one blob. Start at 30 and adjust. The ideal range is dataset-size dependent: try perplexity ≈ N^0.5 / 3.
Skipping PCA pre-reduction on large datasets:
Running t-SNE directly on 768-dim embeddings for 100k points takes hours. Apply PCA to 50 dimensions first — PCA preserves global variance while reducing noise dimensions.
Two separate t-SNE runs are not comparable — the coordinate systems are arbitrary. If you want to compare before/after fine-tuning, use the same random seed and re-embed both together in one run.
Using default UMAP metric='euclidean' for cosine-normalised embeddings:

Sentence transformers produce L2-normalised vectors where cosine similarity equals dot product. Use metric='cosine' or metric='correlation' for UMAP, or pre-normalise and use Euclidean distance on the unit sphere.
Both t-SNE and UMAP use random initialisation. Always set random_state=42 (or any constant) so that plots are reproducible.
Apply PCA before t-SNE for large datasets:
PCA(n_components=50) then TSNE(n_components=2) is the standard pipeline for N > 10,000. It is faster and often produces cleaner clusters.
Use UMAP when you need to embed new points:
t-SNE has no transform() method — you cannot add new points without re-running the full algorithm. UMAP's .transform() embeds new points in under a second.
Plot at perplexity ∈ {5, 30, 100} for t-SNE or n_neighbors ∈ {5, 15, 50} for UMAP. Cluster structure that appears consistently across settings is trustworthy; structure that varies is an artifact.
Make separate plots coloured by label, length, timestamp, and confidence score. Patterns in unexpected variables (e.g. text length correlates with cluster position) reveal data biases.

# GPU-accelerated UMAP for 1M+ embeddings
# pip install cuml-cu12  # requires CUDA 12

from cuml.manifold import UMAP as cuUMAP
import cupy as cp

embeddings_gpu = cp.asarray(embeddings)  # move to GPU
umap_gpu = cuUMAP(n_components=2, n_neighbors=15, min_dist=0.1, metric="cosine")
xy_gpu = umap_gpu.fit_transform(embeddings_gpu)
xy = cp.asnumpy(xy_gpu)  # back to CPU for plotting
Baseline t-SNE; easy to install; slow for > 50k points
Faster t-SNE with FIt-SNE algorithm; supports transform()
Reference UMAP implementation; pip install umap-learn
GPU-accelerated UMAP; 50–200x faster for large datasets
Interactive cloud-hosted embedding explorer; uploads embeddings and returns shareable 2D map
Log embeddings + metadata; built-in t-SNE/UMAP projector in the UI
3D interactive embedding explorer; integrates with TF/PyTorch training loops
When you need a trained encoder

Embedding visualisation itself introduces minimal security risk, but the embeddings it visualises do. Visualising embeddings of sensitive documents (medical records, financial data) creates a human-readable map of that data's structure. An attacker with access to a t-SNE plot could infer document categories, topic distributions, or even individual document content from cluster positions.
Never publish embedding plots of sensitive data:
A t-SNE of healthcare embeddings labelled by diagnosis is effectively a patient record leak. Remove or aggregate labels before sharing plots externally.
Visualisation does not validate the embedding model:

A plot with clean, well-separated clusters looks convincing but does not prove the model will generalise. Always measure downstream task performance (retrieval NDCG, classification accuracy) alongside visualisation.
If you are using plots as evidence in a report or paper, document the exact random_state, hyperparameters, and library version. t-SNE results are sensitive to all three.
uses t-SNE and UMAP to project high-dimensional embeddings into 2D/3D so humans can see structure, clusters, and relationships.
excels at clear, tight local clusters;
is faster, preserves more global structure, and supports new data.
, it’s used for exploring embeddings and latent spaces; in
, it’s used for visualizing LLM embeddings, latent spaces, and model behavior.
The workflow is: get embeddings → apply t-SNE/UMAP → color and plot → interpret clusters and structure.
